file_name: Alhaj and Ozturk - 2026 - GC-ViT-E Enhanced global context vision transformers for robust skin cancer classification under se.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses enhanced global context vision transformers (GC-ViT-E), specifically evaluating architectures like GC-ViT Small, CoAt-Lite, and FocalNet augmented with Transformer Blocks and Separable Self-Attention modules. These deep learning models were trained and evaluated on dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 and ISIC 2019 datasets to perform multi-class skin cancer classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a novel architecture (GC-ViT-E) using specific datasets (HAM10000 and ISIC 2019). It includes detailed methods (preprocessing, data augmentation, model architecture), results (accuracy, macro-F1, macro-AUC metrics), ablation studies, and statistical significance testing, which are characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: GC-ViT-E: Enhanced Global Context Vision Transformers for Robust Skin Cancer Classification Under Severe Class Imbalance
journal: Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences
brief_summary: This study addresses robust multi-class skin cancer classification under severe class imbalance by proposing GC-ViT-E, an enhanced global context vision transformer architecture. The authors evaluated three base architectures—GC-ViT Small, CoAt-Lite, and FocalNet—augmented with Transformer Blocks for global context and Separable Self-Attention for precision-recall calibration. Models were trained and tested on the HAM10000 and ISIC 2019 datasets using a stratified 70/15/15 split protocol. GC-ViT-E achieved 92.81% accuracy on HAM10000 and 91.42% on ISIC 2019, outperforming hierarchical alternatives by 2.5–4.0 percentage points in accuracy and 3.5–6.4 in macro-F1. The results demonstrate that explicit global attention and attention factorization significantly improve rare-class recognition and transfer robustness in dermoscopic classification.

In ISIC 2019, it is also the most common class (12,875 total images). GC-ViT-E achieved high F1-scores for this class in both datasets (0.9625 on HAM10000, 0.9499 on ISIC 2019).

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin cancer diagnostic categories (e.g., Melanocytic nevi, Melanoma, Basal cell carcinoma, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class skin cancer classification using dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 and ISIC 2019 datasets. The models predict discrete diagnostic labels for seven or eight lesion classes. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, macro-F1, macro-Precision, macro-Recall, MCC, Cohen’s kappa, and macro-AUC. The task addresses severe class imbalance among these discrete categories.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The "Human Against Machine with 10,000 training images" dataset, comprising 10,015 dermoscopic images annotated across seven clinically relevant diagnostic categories. Diagnostic labels are primarily established through histopathological examination, with additional validation via longitudinal clinical follow-up, multidisciplinary expert consensus, and in vivo reflectance confocal microscopy. Originating from multiple clinical centers and imaging devices.

use.training: Used for training models under a stratified 70/15/15 split protocol.
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter tuning, model selection, and early stopping under a stratified 70/15/15 split protocol.
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation on the held-out test subset under a stratified 70/15/15 split protocol.
use.other: Used as a target domain in cross-dataset transfer experiments (testing on HAM10000 after training on ISIC 2019).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: The ISIC 2019 challenge dataset, expanding scale and diagnostic scope with 25,331 training images across eight lesion classes. The study used publicly available labeled images and constructed internal stratified partitions rather than relying on challenge-server evaluation.

use.training: Used for training models under a stratified 70/15/15 split protocol.
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter tuning, model selection, and early stopping under a stratified 70/15/15 split protocol.
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation on the held-out test subset under a stratified 70/15/15 split protocol.
use.other: Used as a source domain in cross-dataset transfer experiments (training on ISIC 2019 and testing on HAM10000). SCC was excluded from ISIC 2019 for cross-dataset experiments to align label spaces with HAM10000.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantify uncertainty for all reported classification metrics (Accuracy, macro-F1, macro-Precision, macro-Recall, MCC, Cohen’s kappa, macro-AUC) by resampling the held-out test set with replacement.
To assess statistical significance of pairwise differences in accuracy between models on the held-out test set.
To assess statistical significance of pairwise differences in macro-F1 scores between models on the held-out test set.
To adjust p-values for multiple comparisons in the pairwise statistical significance analysis.

hypothesis_testing.test: Paired McNemar’s test (for accuracy) and Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test (for macro-F1, macro-Precision, macro-Recall, MCC, Cohen’s kappa), with Holm–Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons.
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no statistically significant difference in performance between the compared models (e.g., GC-ViT-E vs. CoAt-Lite-E or FocalNet-E) on the respective metric.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference in performance between the compared models on the respective metric.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: On the HAM10000 dataset, pairwise differences in accuracy and macro-F1 did not reach statistical significance after Holm–Bonferroni correction (e.g., adjusted McNemar’s p=0.2499 for GC-ViT-E vs. CoAt-Lite-E accuracy; adjusted Wilcoxon p=0.1873 for macro-F1). On the ISIC 2019 dataset, GC-ViT-E’s accuracy advantages over CoAt-Lite-E and FocalNet-E were highly statistically significant (adjusted McNemar’s p < 0.001), and gains in macro-F1, MCC, and Cohen’s kappa were also statistically significant (adjusted Wilcoxon p < 0.05).

HAM10000.notes: GC-ViT-E improved accuracy by 2.46 pp over CoAt-Lite-E and 2.72 pp over FocalNet-E. Differences were not statistically significant after Holm-Bonferroni correction.

ISIC 2019.notes: GC-ViT-E improved accuracy by ~3.9 pp and macro-F1 by 5.08–6.38 pp over baselines. Improvements were statistically significant.

Cross-Dataset Transfer.experiment: Training on HAM10000, Testing on ISIC 2019
Cross-Dataset Transfer.notes: Significant drop in performance indicates directional asymmetry and difficulty in generalizing from less diverse source domain.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported for the specific GC-ViT-E models in the provided text; context mentions ImageNet channel statistics normalization but not weight initialization source.

augmentation_modules: Transformer Blocks (two pre-norm Transformer blocks for global contextual coupling)
Separable Self-Attention (SSA) module (depthwise-pointwise token mixing prior to attention)

transformer_block_details.residual_connections: Applied after attention output and after feed-forward network

ssa_details.token_mixing: Depthwise 1-D convolution (kernel size 3, groups=C) followed by pointwise 1x1 projection

training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Two-stage policy with linear warm-up over the first five epochs

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied at the image level to modulate the effective field of view without erasing lesion structure.
Applied as a compact, policy-based family of mild geometric and photometric adjustments to emulate differences in illumination, contrast, and capture conditions.
Applied at the image level; described as label-preserving in dermoscopy.
Applied at the batch level as convex mixing of samples to enlarge the effective support around each training instance.
Applied at the batch level as region-level patch mixing to encourage smoother decision boundaries.
Applied to reduce over-confidence and promote more equitable learning for minority categories.

conclusions: GC-ViT-E, an enhanced global context vision transformer augmented with Transformer Blocks and Separable Self-Attention, achieved superior multi-class skin cancer classification performance compared to hierarchical alternatives (CoAt-Lite-E and FocalNet-E).

On the HAM10000 dataset, GC-ViT-E achieved 92.81% accuracy and 0.9029 macro-F1, outperforming baselines by 2.46–2.72 percentage points in accuracy, though these gains were not statistically significant after Holm–Bonferroni correction.

On the ISIC 2019 dataset, GC-ViT-E achieved 91.42% accuracy and 0.8690 macro-F1, significantly outperforming baselines by approximately 3.9 percentage points in accuracy and 5.08–6.38 percentage points in macro-F1 (adjusted McNemar’s p < 0.001; adjusted Wilcoxon p < 0.05).

The architectural enhancements specifically improved rare-class recognition and balanced multi-class decision quality, with GC-ViT-E maintaining high F1-scores for underrepresented lesions such as dermatofibroma and vascular lesions.
Ablation studies indicated that Transformer Blocks were primarily responsible for recall gains, while Separable Self-Attention mainly refined the precision–recall balance.

Cross-dataset transfer experiments revealed pronounced directional asymmetry: training on ISIC 2019 and testing on HAM10000 yielded high performance (97.54% accuracy), whereas training on HAM10000 and testing on ISIC 2019 resulted in a significant drop (77.49% accuracy), underscoring the critical importance of source-domain diversity for external generalization.
important_remarks: The manuscript is an unedited 'Article in Press' version; the authors explicitly warn that errors may be present and that the content may change before final publication.

Statistical significance of performance gains on the HAM10000 dataset was not achieved after Holm-Bonferroni correction (adjusted McNemar’s p=0.2499 for accuracy; adjusted Wilcoxon p=0.1873 for macro-F1), despite numerical improvements over baselines.

Cross-dataset transfer experiments revealed pronounced directional asymmetry: training on ISIC 2019 and testing on HAM10000 yielded 97.54% accuracy, whereas the reverse direction dropped to 77.49% accuracy, indicating strong source-domain dependency.

The study constructed internal stratified train/validation/test partitions (70/15/15) for the ISIC 2019 dataset rather than using the official challenge-server evaluation protocol, which limits direct comparability with prior ISIC 2019 benchmark results.

Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) was excluded from the cross-dataset transfer experiments to align label spaces with HAM10000, limiting the assessment of generalization to this specific malignant class in transfer settings.

Performance on the smallest classes (e.g., Vascular lesions and Dermatofibroma) carries wider uncertainty due to limited held-out test support, particularly in HAM10000 where these classes have very few samples.

The model exhibited a conservative failure pattern for Dermatofibroma on ISIC 2019, characterized by very high precision (0.9600) but substantially reduced recall (0.6667), indicating a tendency to miss true cases rather than over-predict.
